analogy: Think of "International Organisations" through the chapter's own opening images: O verview This chapter examines the growing significance of environmental as well as resource issues in world politics. It analyses in a comparative perspective some of the important environmental movements against the backdrop of the rising profile of environmentalism from the 1960s onwar ds. Notions... The easy idea is to follow the chapter's own clues one by one instead of memorising a loose definition. First notice the words and examples the text repeats: 83 Environment and N atural R esources, 85 Environment and N atural R esources, ANTARCTICA, 87 Environment and N atural R esources. Then connect each example to the question the chapter is trying to answer. Like solving a source-backed puzzle, every useful point must come from the chapter text itself and stay traceable to the NCERT source.
full_notes:
International Organisations is decoded here only from the NCERT chapter text. The chapter begins with its own framing, examples and questions, then builds the topic through source-backed facts. The notes below keep that order: first the opening problem, then the important terms, then the mechanisms, examples and exam-useful connections that appear in the chapter itself.

The text explains that o verview This chapter examines the growing significance of environmental as well as resource issues in world politics.

The text explains that it analyses in a comparative perspective some of the important environmental movements against the backdrop of the rising profile of environmentalism from the 1960s onwar ds.

The text explains that notions of common property resources and the global commons too are assessed.

The text explains that we also discuss, in brief, the stand taken by India in more recent environmental debates.

The text explains that next follows a brief account of the geopolitics of resource competition.

The text explains that we conclude by taking note of the indigenous peoples’ voices and concerns from the margins of contemporary world politics.

The text explains that chapter 6 Environment and Natural Resources The 1992 Earth Summit has brought environmental issues to the centre-stage of global politics.

The text explains that the pictures above show rainforest and mangroves.

The text explains that contemporary World Politics 82 E nvironmEntal ConCErns in Global PolitiCs In this book we have discussed ‘world politics’ in a fairly limited sense: wars and treaties, rise and decline of state power, the relationship between the governments that represent their countries in the international arena and the role of inter- governmental organisations.

The text explains that in Chapter 5, we expanded the scope of world politics to include issues like poverty and epidemics.

The text explains that that may not have been a very difficult step to take, for we all think that governments are responsible for controlling these.

The text explains that in that sense they fall within the scope of world politics.

The text explains that do you think they fall within the scope of contemporary world politics?

The text explains that t h r o u g h o u t t h e w o r l d , cultivable area is barely expanding any more, and a substantial portion of existing agricultural land is losing fertility.

The text explains that grasslands have been overgrazed and fisheries over- harvested.

The text explains that water bodies have suffered extensive depletion a n d p o l l u t i o n , s e v e r e l y restricting food production.

The text explains that a steady decline in the total amount of ozone in the Earth’s s t r a t o s p h e r e ( c o m m o n l y referred to as the ozone hole) poses a real danger to ecosystems and human health.

The text explains that 83 Environment and N atural R esources becoming increasingly polluted largely due to land-based activities.

The text explains that if the various governments take steps to check e n v i r o n m e n t a l d e g r a d a t i o n of the kind mentioned above, these issues will have political consequences in that sense.

The text explains that most of them are such that no single government can address them fully.

The text explains that issues of environment and natural resources are political in another deeper sense.

The text explains that who gets to use how much of the natural resources of the Earth?

The text explains that they are, therefore, deeply political questions.

The text explains that a l t h o u g h e n v i r o n m e n t a l concerns have a long history, awareness of the environmental consequences of economic growth acquired an increasingly political character from the 1960s onwards.

The text explains that the Club of Rome, a global think tank, published a book in 1972 entitled Limits to Growth, dramatising the potential depletion of the Earth’s resources against the backdrop of rapidly growing world population.

The text explains that let us look at some of the contentious issues in the global politics of environment. thE ProtECtion of Global Commons ‘Commons’ are those resources which are not owned by anyone but rather shared by a community.

The text explains that similarly, there are some Are there different perspectives from which the rich and the poor countries agree to protect the Earth? © Ares, Cagle Cartoons Inc.

The text explains that 85 Environment and N atural R esources areas or regions of the world which are located outside the sovereign jurisdiction of any one state, and therefore require common governance by the international community.

The text explains that they include the earth’s atmosphere, Antarctica (see Box), the ocean floor, and outer space.

The text explains that it has a limited terrestrial life and a highly productive marine ecosystem, comprising a few plants (e.g. microscopic algae, fungi and lichen), marine mammals, fish and hordes of birds adapted to harsh conditions, as well as the krill, which is central to marine food chain and upon which other animals are dependent.

The text explains that the Antarctic plays an important role in maintaining climatic equilibrium, and deep ice cores provide an important source of information about greenhouse gas concentrations and atmospheric temperatures of hundreds and thousands of years ago.

The text explains that some countries like the UK, Argentina, Chile, Norway, France, Australia and New Zealand have made legal claims to sovereign rights over Antarctic territory.

The text explains that these differences, however, have not prevented the adoption of innovative and potentially far-reaching rules for the protection of the Antarctic environment and its ecosystem.

The text explains that similarly, the history of outer space as a global commons shows that the management of these areas is thoroughly influenced by North-South inequalities.

The text explains that as with the earth’s atmosphere and the ocean floor, the crucial issue here is technology and industrial development.

The text explains that common but DiffErEntiatED rEsPonsibilitiEs We have noted above a difference in the approach to environment between the countries of the North One of the biggest catastrophes in Africa in the 1970s, a drought turned the best cropland in five countries into cracked and barren earth.

The text explains that source: www.gobartimes.org Find out more about the Kyoto Protocol.

The text explains that 87 Environment and N atural R esources and the South.

The text explains that moreover, the developing countries are in the process of industrialisation and they must not be subjected to the same restrictions, which apply to the developed countries.

The text explains that this argument was accepted in the Rio Declaration at the Earth Summit in 1992 and is called the principle of ‘common but differentiated responsibilities’.

The text explains that the relevant part of the Rio Declaration says that “States shall cooperate in the spirit of global partnership to conserve, protect and restore the health and integrity of the Earth’s e c o s y s t e m .

The text explains that the developed c o u n t r i e s a c k n o w l e d g e t h e responsibility that they bear in the international pursuit of sustainable development in view of the pressures their societies place on the global environment and of the technological and financial resources they command.” The 1992 United Nations F r a m e w o r k C o n v e n t i o n o n Climate Change (UNFCCC) also provides that the parties should act to protect the climate system “on the basis of equity and in accordance with their common but differentiated responsibilities and respective capabilities.” The parties to the Convention agreed that the largest share of historical and current global emissions of greenhouse gases has originated in developed countries.

The text explains that certain gases like Carbon dioxide, Methane, Hydro- fluoro carbons etc. are considered at least partly responsible for global warming - the rise in global temperature which may have catastrophic consequences for life on Earth.

The text explains that contemporary World Politics 88 Common ProPErty rEsourCEs Common property represents common property for the group.

The text explains that the underlying norm here is that members of the group have both rights and duties with respect to the nature, levels of use, and the maintenance of a given resource.

The text explains that which among the following are TRUE about the global commons? a.

The text explains that the Earth’s atmosphere, Antarctica, ocean floor and outer space are considered as part of the global commons. b.

The text explains that the global commons are outside sovereign jurisdiction. c.

The text explains that the question of managing the global commons has reflected the North-South divide. d.

The text explains that the countries of the North are more concerned about the protection of the global commons than the countries of the South.

The text explains that what is meant by ‘common but differentiated responsibilities’?

The text explains that why have issues related to global environmental protection become the priority concern of states since the 1990s?

Source: NCERT Class 12 Contemporary World Politics, Chapter 6: International Organisations
